text:
This paper investigates to what extent political parties in Western Europe take into account the priorities of citizens in their election programmes. The existing literature on political congruence has almost exclusively focused on the positional aspect of political representation, whereas this paper investigates the thematic aspect, that is, the extent to which political parties take into account the issues that are salient to voters. Based on a novel research design, the paper investigates the conditions under which thematic political congruence is more likely to emerge. The empirical analysis is based on a large dataset of 130 parties across 18 Western European countries for the period 1972–2011. The data covers all relevant information on the election programmes of the political parties (CMP3 dataset) as well as the responses of voters to the question of what they regard as the most important social problem (MIP dataset).

Since publication of the previous working paper the lead author of the paper has revised it further to incorporate several pieces of feedback from peers. The findings from the original paper are largely confirmed within this revised version with one important qualification. Within the model of part which investigates the relationship between the extent of party responsiveness to the overall population's focus of attention and party characteristics the lead author has introduced greater scope for estimation with only limited changes to the overall conclusions which have been drawn. In particular it was concluded that all things being equal catch-all parties are larger than their smaller counterparts, that governing parties have credibility and policy constraints which depress their levels of responsiveness relative to their opponents, and that niche parties who in general do not appear more responsive to public opinion are however considerably more responsive to the concerns of the highly focused sub-groups within their support base -- particularly where such parties exist within a particular issue domain: examples here might include environmental and animal rights focus for the Greens in Europe. The paper therefore provides much clearer understanding of democratic linkage -- how political parties connect with voters through issue agendas -- and the ways in which parties operate on the field of play within a democratic electoral politics.
